source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
A square metallic frame with side length L and mass m, where each side has a resistance of $R_{0}$, is placed on a smooth, insulated horizontal table (the plane of the paper). A uniform magnetic field with a magnetic flux density of magnitude $B$ is present within a region of width 2L, oriented perpendicularly to the plane of the paper. The two dashed lines indicate the boundaries of the magnetic field, as shown in Figure (a).

1. The metallic frame is given an initial velocity to the right and enters the magnetic field. During the motion, the left and right sides of the frame remain parallel to the magnetic field boundaries. After the frame has completely passed through the magnetic field region, its speed decreases to half of its initial speed. Determine the magnitude of the initial velocity of the metallic frame.
2. Two smooth, long, straight metallic rails are fixed on the table, perpendicular to the magnetic field boundaries. A resistor $R_{1}=2R_{0}$ is connected to the left end of the rails. The resistance of the rails is negligible. The metallic frame is placed on the rails, as shown in Figure (b). The frame is given the same initial velocity as in (1) to the right, and it enters the magnetic field. During the motion, the upper and lower sides of the frame remain in good electrical contact with the rails at all times. Calculate the amount of heat generated in the resistor $R_{1}$ during the entire motion of the metallic frame.

image_dependency: requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate the problem is primarily figure-supported
question text explicitly references Figure (a) and Figure (b) and uses dashed boundaries/rail placement shown in the figure
solving depends on spatial layout: frame orientation relative to magnetic-field boundaries and rail contacts during motion
multi_step: the prompt has two linked subproblems, with part (2) explicitly using the same initial velocity found in part (1)
the setup combines geometry, electromagnetic induction, circuit resistance, motion, and energy/heat computation rather than a single direct lookup
the motion occurs through different field-entry/exit configurations, implying stage-based reasoning across the trajectory
verifiability: answer_type=short_text with two closed-form algebraic results makes target outputs specific
answer_text provides explicit symbolic expressions, so a checker can compare for mathematical equivalence
registry_passed=true, question_present=true, and answer_present=true reduce asset-level uncertainty

decision_reasons: Asset registry is complete: question, answer, and primary image are all present, and registry_passed=true.
Initial scoring is strong, with high image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability scores.
The problem appears to retain full core semantics because the figure-supported setup, detailed text, and explicit short-text answer are all available.
Risk flags are manageable follow-up concerns rather than evidence of missing core assets or unrecoverable semantics.

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement is complete and the diagram clearly shows both setups, the magnetic-field region of width 2L, and the rail/resistor arrangement needed to interpret the two sub-questions. The provided answers align with the two requested quantities.

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is complete and understandable, the diagram-text relationship is clear, both target answers are present, and the rewrite/alignment/solvability evidence shows no serious issues for downstream annotation.

field_audit_records.before_value:
A square metallic frame with side length L and mass m, where each side has a resistance of $R_{0}$, is placed on a smooth, insulated horizontal table (the plane of the paper). A uniform magnetic field with a magnetic flux density of magnitude $B$ is present within a region of width 2L, oriented perpendicularly to the plane of the paper. The two dashed lines indicate the boundaries of the magnetic field, as shown in Figure (a).

1. The metallic frame is given an initial velocity to the right and enters the magnetic field. During the motion, the left and right sides of the frame remain parallel to the magnetic field boundaries. After the frame has completely passed through the magnetic field region, its speed decreases to half of its initial speed. Determine the magnitude of the initial velocity of the metallic frame.
2. Two smooth, long, straight metallic rails are fixed on the table, perpendicular to the magnetic field boundaries. A resistor $R_{1}=2R_{0}$ is connected to the left end of the rails. The resistance of the rails is negligible. The metallic frame is placed on the rails, as shown in Figure (b). The frame is given the same initial velocity as in (1) to the right, and it enters the magnetic field. During the motion, the upper and lower sides of the frame remain in good electrical contact with the rails at all times. Calculate the amount of heat generated in the resistor $R_{1}$ during the entire motion of the metallic frame.
